frameworks.name: continual learning / catastrophic interference
frameworks.one_line: sequential task acquisition where new learning can corrupt prior representations

frameworks.name: deep neural networks (connectionist)
frameworks.one_line: artificial agents compared head-to-head against humans on the same sequential task

frameworks.name: rich vs lazy training regime
frameworks.one_line: regime knob that recreates human individual-difference strategies in networks

frameworks.name: transfer of learning
frameworks.one_line: knowledge from Task A applied to similar Task B

core_question: Do humans and neural networks show similar patterns of transfer and interference when learning Task A, then Task B, then re-tested on Task A?
key_assumptions: transfer and interference can be operationalised within one canonical sequential rule-learning paradigm
hidden representations in networks are interpretable proxies for the learner's solution
training regime (rich vs lazy) maps onto qualitative human strategies
manipulations: task similarity between Task A and Task B (similar vs dissimilar)
key_findings: similar successive tasks yield greater transfer but also greater interference, in both humans and networks
network interference arises because existing solutions are reused and thereby corrupted for prior use
humans split into 'lumpers' (interference, better generalisation) and 'splitters' (no interference, better feature memory)
rich vs lazy training regimes recreate the lumper/splitter individual differences in networks
interpretations: human and network continual-learning behaviour share a common transfer/interference trade-off
individual strategy differences reflect the same representational regime that distinguishes rich from lazy network learning
